skill.name: context-compression
skill.description: Design and evaluate compression strategies for long-running sessions
skill.summary: Optimize AI agent context management using structured summarization and probe-based evaluation to reduce token costs while preserving critical information.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All 16 static analysis findings are false positives. The skill contains only markdown documentation (SKILL.md, 271 lines) with no executable code. Detected patterns include markdown code block delimiters misidentified as shell execution, documentation URLs misidentified as network calls, and conceptual references misidentified as cryptographic implementations. No security risks identified.

content.user_title: Optimize AI context compression for long-running sessions
content.value_statement: AI agents waste tokens re-fetching lost context after aggressive compression. This skill teaches structured summarization strategies that preserve critical information while reducing token costs by up to 98%.
actual_capabilities: Design anchored iterative summarization with explicit file-tracking sections
Implement probe-based evaluation to measure compression quality
Apply three-phase compression workflow for large codebases
Choose appropriate compression strategy based on task requirements
Track artifact trails across compression cycles
Optimize for tokens-per-task instead of tokens-per-request
limitations: Does not provide ready-to-use compression code implementations
Requires manual integration with your agent scaffolding
Evaluation framework requires custom probe question design
File tracking remains challenging even with structured approaches

use_cases.title: Debug long-running agent sessions
use_cases.description: Maintain context across 100+ message debugging sessions while tracking file modifications, error messages, and decisions without losing critical technical details.
use_cases.target_user: Software developers using Claude Code for complex debugging tasks

use_cases.title: Build enterprise coding agents
use_cases.description: Design context management for agents working with 5M+ token codebases, implementing compression that preserves function signatures, type definitions, and architectural decisions.
use_cases.target_user: AI platform engineers building production agent systems

use_cases.title: Evaluate compression quality
use_cases.description: Test different compression strategies using probe-based evaluation to measure accuracy, artifact tracking, and continuation capability before deploying to production.

prompt_templates.title: Beginner: Design a structured summary template
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a structured summary template for my coding agent sessions. I need sections for tracking modified files, decisions made, and next steps. My agent works primarily with TypeScript and Redis.
prompt_templates.scenario: Starting with context compression for the first time

prompt_templates.title: Intermediate: Implement compression triggers
prompt_templates.prompt: I'm building a coding agent that loses context after 50 messages. Design a compression trigger strategy using the sliding window approach. Include criteria for when to compress and how to merge summaries incrementally.
prompt_templates.scenario: Implementing compression in an existing agent

prompt_templates.title: Advanced: Evaluate compression quality
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a probe-based evaluation framework for my context compression system. Generate 20 probe questions across recall, artifact tracking, continuation, and decision categories to test if my summaries preserve critical information.
prompt_templates.scenario: Testing compression quality before production deployment

prompt_templates.title: Expert: Optimize for large codebases
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a three-phase compression workflow for a 5M token codebase migration project. Include research phase compression to specification, planning phase to implementation details, and strategies for using example artifacts as compression seeds.

output_examples.input: Agent session with 178 messages debugging authentication errors
output: Structured summary created with 5 sections: Session Intent, Root Cause, Files Modified, Test Status, Next Steps
Probe evaluation: 4.2/5 accuracy on technical detail retention

output_examples.input: Comparison of three compression strategies for coding tasks
output: Anchored Iterative: 98.6% compression, 3.70 quality score - Best for file tracking
Regenerative: 98.7% compression, 3.44 quality score - Good balance
Opaque: 99.3% compression, 3.35 quality score - Highest compression, lowest quality

best_practices: Use explicit summary sections for file modifications, decisions, and next steps to prevent silent information loss
Trigger compression at 70-80% context utilization with incremental merging rather than full regeneration
Test compression quality with probe questions before deploying to production workflows
anti_patterns: Optimizing for tokens-per-request instead of tokens-per-task total cost
Using aggressive opaque compression when file tracking is critical for the task
Regenerating full summaries on each compression instead of incrementally merging

faq.question: What compression ratio should I expect for coding agent sessions?
faq.answer: Structured summarization achieves 98.6% compression while maintaining quality. For an 89000 token session, expect approximately 1240 tokens after compression. Opaque methods reach 99.3% but lose critical technical details.

faq.question: How do I know if my compression is losing important information?
faq.answer: Use probe-based evaluation: ask your agent questions about file paths, error messages, and decisions after compression. If answers are vague or incorrect, your compression loses signal. Track re-fetching frequency as a quality indicator.

faq.question: What sections should I include in a structured summary for coding tasks?
faq.answer: Include Session Intent, Files Modified with specific changes, Decisions Made, Current State with test results, and Next Steps. These sections act as checklists that force the summarizer to preserve critical information.

faq.question: When should I compress my agent conversation context?
faq.answer: Trigger compression at 70-80% context utilization for predictable behavior. Task-boundary compression (at logical completion points) produces cleaner summaries but has unpredictable timing. Avoid waiting until context limits are hit.

faq.question: Can compression handle multi-file codebase changes?
faq.answer: Yes, but artifact trail tracking is the weakest dimension across all methods (2.2-2.5 out of 5.0). Use explicit file sections in structured summaries and consider separate artifact indexing for critical file-state tracking.

faq.question: Is anchored iterative summarization better than regenerative approaches?
faq.answer: For long-running coding sessions (100+ messages), anchored iterative is superior because it preserves information across cycles through incremental merging. Regenerative approaches may lose details when full context review is not feasible.
